title: Nonequilibrium Fluctuation-Response Theory in the Frequency Domain
authors: Euijoon Kwon, Hyun-Myung Chun, Hyunggyu Park, Jae Sung Lee
abstract: We develop a unified fluctuation-response theory in the frequency domain for nonequilibrium steady states governed by overdamped Langevin dynamics and Markov jump processes. The relation expresses the power spectrum of general observables exactly as a quadratic form of local responses measured at the same frequency, thereby extending static nonequilibrium fluctuation-response relations to finite frequencies. The decomposition is spatial for Langevin systems and edge-resolved for Markov jump processes, and applies uniformly to state-dependent observables, current-like observables, and their combinations. As consequences of the same identity, we derive frequency-domain response uncertainty relations, kinetic and thermodynamic uncertainty relations, the equilibrium fluctuation-dissipation theorem, and Harada-Sasa-type relations. Applications to stochastic networks and driven diffusive systems illustrate how the theory resolves fluctuation spectra into edge-wise contributions and reveals frequency-dependent tradeoffs between fluctuations, response, and dissipation.
summary: This paper establishes a unified, exact fluctuation-response theory in the frequency domain for both continuous Langevin and discrete Markov jump systems in nonequilibrium steady states. It demonstrates that the power spectrum of any observable can be reconstructed from local linear responses, providing a single foundational identity from which several known thermodynamic uncertainty relations and dissipation relations can be derived.
main_problem: The lack of a unified, exact equality relating spontaneous fluctuations to responses in the frequency domain for nonequilibrium steady states.
main_result: The derivation of a unified fluctuation-response theory in the frequency domain that expresses the power spectrum of general observables as a quadratic form of local responses, serving as a parent relation for various uncertainty relations and dissipation relations.
method: The authors use perturbation theory with local impulsive perturbations, functional derivatives, and the mathematical framework of the excess propagator (the projected resolvent of the Fokker-Planck or Master equation generator).
model_or_system: The theory applies to two classes of nonequilibrium steady states: overdamped Langevin dynamics (continuous-state) and Markov jump processes (discrete-state stochastic networks).
key_observables: Power spectrum of general observables, including state-dependent parts, current-like parts, empirical density, and empirical currents.
important_parameters: Finite frequency (omega), nonequilibrium steady states (NESS), mobility (M), temperature (T), transition rates (W), and entropy production rate (sigma).
main_assumptions: The systems are assumed to be in nonequilibrium steady states, the dynamics are limited to overdamped Langevin or Markov jump processes, and the theory relies on linear response to small perturbations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a hierarchy diagram illustrating how the central frequency-domain FRR acts as a foundation for deriving various inequalities (RUR, TUR) and equalities (FDT, Harada-Sasa). Figure 5 illustrates the continuous interpolation between the covariance spectrum and the entropy production rate in a linear setting.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of frequency-domain FRRs, develops the unified theory for Langevin systems, extends the framework to Markov jump processes, derives various consequences (RUR, TUR, FDT, Harada-Sasa) using the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, and provides rigorous mathematical proofs in the appendix.
why_interesting: This provides a rigorous framework for understanding frequency-dependent fluctuations and dissipation, which is directly applicable to the study of open quantum systems and the dynamics of driven many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. A hierarchy of the relations derived in this work. The frequency-domain FRR sits at the top of the hierarchy as the foundational relation. Blue boxes denote exact equalities, while green boxes represent inequalities. Each arrow indicates how a lower-level relation is obtained from a higher-level one. Equation numbers are colored in red for overdamped Langevin systems and in purple for Markov jump processes. The dotted box indicates a relation that reduces to previously reported results in appropriate limits (Refs. [35, 36]).

FIG. 2. Illustration of the frequency-domain FRR for a unicyclic network [Eq. (129)] for (a,c,e,g) state-dependent observable η1(t) and for (b,d,f,h) current-like observable ȷ21(t). Black lines denote the total fluctuation spectrum Cθ,θ(ω) and colored lines represent the edge-wise contributions Cnm θ,θ (ω). Colors indicate the edge index, as shown by the color bar in panel (a). For example, i = 8 corresponds to the edge connecting E8 and E9. In panels (a-d) and (g,h), gray dotted vertical lines indicate the frequency at which Cθ,θ(ω) is maximized. Parameters: k = 1 for all panels. (a-f) N = 20, (g,h) N = 70. (a,b,g,h) F/N = 2, (c,d) F/N = 20, (e,f) F/N = 0.2

FIG. 4. A numerical illustration of the frequency-domain RKUR and TUR for a particle in a tilted potential. (a) Fourier transform of the velocity autocorrelation function. (b) Real (solid) and imaginary (dotted) parts of the Fourier- transformed response function. (c) Quality factor of the frequency-domain RKUR. (d) Quality factor of the frequency- domain TUR. In each panel, lines of different colors corre- spond to different values of f0, as indicated by the color map in (a). Parameters: µ = 1, T = 1, U0 = 2, and L = 2π. Sim- ulations are performed using the Euler–Maruyama method to integrate Eq. (133), with time step dt = 0.001, maximum time tmax = 50, and ensemble size Nens = 105.

FIG. 3. Illustration of the frequency-domain FRR in the KaiC network. (a) KaiC cycle with 14 states. Ki and ¯Ki de- note the active and inactive states with i phosphates, respec- tively. (b-d) Contributions of individual edges to the FRR. In each panel, black lines denote the total fluctuation spec- trum Cθ,θ(ω) of the phosphorylation level P(t), while colored lines denote the contribution from each edge Cnm θ (ω). (b) Contributions from edges between day states. (c) Contribu- tions from edges between night states. (d) Contributions from edges linking day and night states.

FIG. 5. A numerical illustration of Eq. (142) for the linear Langevin system [Eq. (140)] for dimensions (a) N = 2 and (b) N = 4.
